sentences.text: Yeah.
Well, anyway, I wanted to tell you about the army.
Aspirin and a cup of coffee.
I can make you a cup of coffee.
Yeah.
But the Jewish children in that age instead of being trained for the army, they have to wear shovels in their back and go and do all kind of dirty works.
At the same time, when the other Christian children were training for the army, the Jewish children went and cleaned bathrooms in the schools and worked with shovels in the field.
So it was very degrading to-- that they made such a differentiation between the Jewish children and the Gentile children.
So it was very difficult to accept it.
I always had a strong feeling towards my fellow man and a lot of love.
It was hard to understand why.
Just because-- oh.
We weren't allowed to say-- if we would say the least thing, the guards-- the one who did the training-- whatever you call them, the Hungarians-- they would beat us.
We weren't allowed to question anything about it.
Well, when the Germans occupied the part.
And that was in 1943.
Yeah.
And they slowly started to get us together, and took us so-called of a ghetto.
It was in the-- in the brick factory.
In the city, they had a brick factory where they were manufacturing red brick.
And they had the place which had a roof on it, but had no sides.
They had a, what?
A roof on it.
A roof on it, but there was no sides to it.
That's where the brick would dry.
Yeah.
And that's the place we were all taken in.
One day, they just came into our home.
And whatever you were in-- if you were in your pyjamas, that's the way you had to go.
You weren't allowed to take anything.
And they took-- put us into so-called ghetto, to that brick factory.
And we-- [CLEARS THROAT] we stayed there for a few months until they transferred us to Auschwitz.
Well, the first place, we were freezing because it was the spring-- the early spring.
And we were practically freezing there.
It was very cold.
And we just were rationed a certain amount of food.
And we weren't allowed to go to the city anymore.
And a lot of sick children.
And they had no-- nobody was caring to take care of them.
And some of the people had gotten-- lost their marbles.
And I was working there to help, like a nurse-- volunteered as a nurse to help.
So I was taking care of those people who went berserk, kind of.
They couldn't take it.
And I was wearing a white jacket, so they thought I was a doctor.
And I always had a lot of compassion towards people, and somehow I was able to communicate with them to calm them down.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah, that's what it was.
Yeah, it was enclosed.
Well, it wasn't barbed wire--
--but it was enclosed.
You know, it was so long ago that I have a difficult time to actually remember exactly how it was.
But I know that we were closed, and we weren't able to get in.
And there was a lot of guards all around--
--to guard us.
Oh, yeah.
As I said, that's what I did.
I did nursing and all that and helped the sick people.
They had like, an infirmary there.
And that's what I did.
Well, I had my sister and my mother.
They didn't do-- they didn't do much.
They were just sitting and waiting for our destiny.
Well, once a day or twice a day, they fed you.
With bread and some soup.
No.
No, just-- just enough to live and not to die.
There was terrible sanitation.
Terrible.
As I said, it's very difficult to--
--difficult to remember.
But the sanitation, I know that was terrible.
Absolutely.
We had no place to bathe.
Even water was very difficult to-- you know, they didn't have the facilities.
You had to bring the water in buckets.
They didn't have plumbing or anything like that.
And the bathrooms, they had no bathrooms.
You know?
You know It was bad.
That's all I can say.
Very bad.
But we were in sort of in a trance.
We didn't know what's-- where we are or what we're doing, that such a thing is possible to happen, that they treat people like this.
So it was very hard.
Some of the Hungarian soldiers.
Yes.
Mm-hm.
The German and the Hungarian.
A few months.
I don't know the exact time.
And then they put us into wagons and transported us to Auschwitz.
Well, that was a terrible journey, what we had in that train.
It was more like cattle trains.
And we was-- we were squeezed in so close, so many people to one train that we couldn't-- couldn't even move.
There was all my cousins, my aunts, and their children and everybody.
I have no idea.
I know one thing-- that we couldn't move.
You have to practically step on somebody in order to-- you couldn't move anywhere.
I believe it was about four, five days on a train.
And then we arrived to Auschwitz.
Well, it was-- as I said, mother and my mother's sister, and my cousins and my cousins' children.
And they were sick.
And they were hollering.
And it's very difficult to describe the whole thing.
It didn't seem real, that these things was possible to happen, that this is really happening to us, to be treated like animals.
And so it was a very tough journey until we arrived to Auschwitz, and not to know where we're going, what we-- what's going to be with us.
And you still, even with-- you had no future.
You can see it.
You had that much intelligence to realize that if they treat you that way, that your future doesn't look good.
But we still lived with some hopes.

You can't believe that such a thing is possible, that the 20th century when people are supposed to be the highest peak as far as intelligence is concerned-- that they can do something like that to other person.
So we still lived with hopes that something-- even that we knew that it doesn't look good.
So we finally arrived to Auschwitz.
And then they were whispering to us, don't go with your parents.
Who was whispering that?
Some of the-- some of the people in Auschwitz who-- some prisoners--
Who was there before us, and they were working there.
If they had a chance, because there was the SS with the rifles, and one to go to left, one to go to right.
But sometimes, when they had a chance to tell you, don't take your kids with you.
They told some of the people--
So that's what we did.
We didn't know what we were doing.
We just-- everything went so fast and so unbelievable that you just went along.
They told you to go to the right or left, you went.
And you did what they told you to do.
And where did you go?
So I was taken into a barrack with all the wires around you.
And we were pushed in.
I don't know how many thousand people to one of those barracks.
But again, we were squeezed in like sardines.
You know?
And we still didn't know-- didn't comprehend what's there, what's going to be our future, or what's happening.
We couldn't believe it.
No, no, no.
No, we were--
We were separated.
That was the last I seen them.
We were separated as soon as we got down from the train.
The children were separated.
And they had us--
So as I said, [CLEARS THROAT], it was just difficult to believe that-- we didn't know.
It seemed to me like today they're talking about outer space-- that we are somebody in outer space.
And so when we woke up in the morning, and I went out front of the barrack when we were allowed to go out, always a big pile of dead people was piled top on each other.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
As I said, I was in such a state of mind that I couldn't believe that such a thing is possible.
What is this?
I just couldn't-- the big pile every single day in front of it.
So we stayed there.
I was there for about a week at that time, I believe.
And naturally, they gave you once a day a bowl-- something, some kind of a cereal or something.
It was-- I don't know.
And we didn't even have spoons.
We just hand it to each other, and everybody slurped it down from that plate.
They had no silverware or anything-- spoons or anything-- to eat with.
And that's the way it was.
And I-- [PAUSES FOR 23 SECONDS] A lot of peoples' eyes was-- [CRYING] They were all swollen, their eyes and everything.
No.
They didn't make you work there at all.
We were waiting to get some numbers.
They were giving you the numbers.
And they said that if they need you for work, they'll take you.
If not, then they would put you to the gas chamber and gas you out.
No.
I mean, we heard it from the people who were already in there.
No, I didn't have the chance to get a number before they supposed to-- there were so many people coming in, too many people at that time from Hungary, the part where I come from.
And there was so many people there already from Poland who suffered even longer-- for years.
I was about 18, 19. 19.
I was liberated when I was 20.
Was it about 20?
Yeah. 20 years old.
So I stayed there for about-- I believe about 10 days or two weeks.
I don't know quite sure.
But I remember one thing, that next to the wire fence of the other barracks, there was about [COUGHS] 35,000 Czechoslovakian people was brought in.
And they all had their kids and their families together.
And I was very jealous that they have their family with them.
And we talked through the wire.
And I happened to meet a gentleman who was coming from the same town that I came from.
He was living at Prague at that time.
And he was telling me, don't be jealous because we're going to be all gassed, killed out.
And this was the people who-- at that time-- came from the Sudeten part of Czechoslovakia-- Lidice-- where they resisted the Germans when they came in there.
Excuse me.
And the-- the whole town was brought in for that reason.
And that was some of the people, the 35,000.
He says, we're going to be all killed.
You still have a chance to--
35,000 People.
That's what I was told.
I'm just quoting what they told me.
It was packed-- children, families together.
And we had, that night-- that was after when I talked to those people there-- and that night, they had a-- what did they used to call now-- when they didn't let you-- or closed you into the barracks.
Curfews, yeah.
And there was a curfew at [INAUDIBLE].
And we heard hollering and crying.
And all the people disappeared.
It was empty the following two days.
They killed all the people out in a couple of nights.
Yes.
Oh, yes.
We were told.
But even that we aware of it, we couldn't believe it, that such a thing-- you know, that we were there to see, and I seen every-- there the big pile of dead people piled up.
You know, it's difficult to believe that even when you see it, that such a thing can exist, such a thing can happen.
So we didn't believe it.
So then, as I said, that was my experience in Auschwitz.
But the whole thing happened in such a short period of time.
I was there for about 10 days, and suddenly they needed people to work.
And they just picked us out from the crowd.
How many people were there in your barrack?
I was already standing to get my--
Number on the arm.
And-- and then they just took you.
And that was the time they took me to Buchenwald from Auschwitz.
Buchenwald.
With a train.
Yeah, the wagons.
And as I said, I arrived to Buchenwald.
And I was in one of those shower rooms.
They said that they use it for showers.
And if it's not showers, they gassed you out if they needed people for work.
That's what I was told.
And we was-- I was sitting there, and somebody had asked me that if you know-- asked my name, if you know such-and-such a fellow.
And I said, that's me.
And that's the way I met my brother-in-law, who was-- who was there from Brussels, Belgium.
So I couldn't believe it.
That was the first day.
First day in Buchenwald, as soon as we arrived.
We had the--
No, that was already from Auschwitz.
We came from Auschwitz.
They took all our of clothes away.
We wear the-- the stripes.
Striped uniform.
Mm-hm.
Pardon me?
They shaved my hair.
And they made us go under the water or something.
It was full of some kind of disinfecting stuff that burned the eyes out and everything.
Yeah, that was Auschwitz.
You have to dunk on the-- first, they shaved you.
I think that they shaved us already in Auschwitz.
But they reshaved us again when we came.
And then, as I said, we have to bathe.
And I remember one thing that I thought I'm going to die from burn, everything.
It was such a strong chemical in the water or something.
They tried to kill lice or whatever they had when I came there.
I knew that I had a brother-in-law.
I never met him, because, as I said, that my sister was married in Brussels, Belgium.
And then the war broke out, and we never had any contact with them.
I knew that-- I knew his name.
And I knew that-- who he was.
Yes, he was Jewish.
But he was taken as a prisoner, as a political prisoner, from Brussels, Belgium, because he was working with an underground movement against Hitler regime.
So-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So as I said, it was--
--very unusual circumstances to meet him.
You know.
And he told me that he's going to try to take care of me and see that maybe they don't take me out, because he was working there as a foreman.
What they call that, I don't know.
In German they had a phrase for it.
In--
In the camp.
In the camp there.
In Buchenwald.
Well, as I said, there was too many people coming at a time in.
So we were-- we were living in tents instead of the barracks.
[SIGHS] It was a terrible experience.
We had no food.
We didn't get any food.
For days.
And we did get something.
We got a piece of bread and a bowl of soup once a day towards the evening.
We didn't get-- barely had any water to drink.
And we were waiting to be-- we knew that we're not going to stay there long, that we're going to be taken out to work somewhere-- either exterminated or taken up to work.
If you were lucky enough, then they took you to work somewhere.
So I was in Buchenwald at that time for about a week.
And my brother-in-law used to come to visit me.
Time to time, he brought me a little extra piece of bread.
And he tried to see that maybe-- [SIGHS], that I should stay there and he would be able to look after me.
But his promise, couldn't keep it because one day they came and they took me to work.
So again, put me into the wagons.
But the main time when I was there, [SIGHING], the people told me that they used to have appells there.
I haven't seen-- we had the appells that you have to stand always.
That they usually have appells there that they would take the hose in a freezing time, and they hosed the people down until they were frozen and died right there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I did see that one of the prisoners supposed to snitch somebody's bread or something.
And the other prisoners took him, and they throw him right into the outhouse.
They didn't have the bathrooms.
They had those outhouses.
They threw him right inside to that outhouse.
That was the most terrible thing to see.
And I mean, you couldn't even climb out from that, because it was quite large for that many people.
So as I said, after a week, they needed people to work, so they took me.
And we went, again, traveled for about three, four days.
And I don't quite remember the names of the towns, because I really was in a haze.
I didn't know where I was or what-- what next.
I just went along whatever I was told to do.
And we arrived to this little town.
And we were all put into the horse barn.
That's where we were living.
A horse barn?
A horse barn.
It was animals-- horses-- was--
Yeah.
And so we stayed there.
And every single day, we went to work in a gasoline refinery.
They used to bomb it all the time, the Americans.
And-- and we went and cleaned up after the bomb.
So it was about four miles from the-- from the gasoline refinery.
And we had to go with the feet.
We had those wooden shoes.
But we weren't allowed to wear the shoes until we were going to the-- to the place, the working place.
We had to put our shoes on our-- our shoulder.
We had to go with bare feet.
That you shouldn't wear it out.
You shouldn't wear it out until we-- you know, except just for the work.
So some of the fathers-- there were young fathers, were about-- I would say-- under 50, 45.
And they had grown sons, like I, myself.
And they were with them.
And we all marched every day.
And some of those fathers, they couldn't walk as well as the youngsters.
So they were beaten every single day until we got there-- four miles, four or five miles, something like that.
And some of the fathers, I seen that their feet got infected.
